items.q: What do interpretivists seek when researching society?
items.why: Students apply positivist goals to interpretivism. Interpretivists (Weber) reject the natural-science model - humans interpret and give meaning, so research must access meaning, not measure behaviour.

items.q: Why do positivists prefer quantitative methods?
items.correct: They produce objective, reliable, generalisable data resembling natural science.
items.misconception: Because numbers are easier to collect than words.
items.why: Students reduce the choice to convenience. Positivism is a philosophical commitment to studying society scientifically - the preference is about EPISTEMOLOGY, not ease.

items.q: What is the 'dark figure' of crime?
items.correct: The amount of crime that is unreported or unrecorded.
items.why: Students take 'dark' literally. It's a methodological concept - official statistics undercount crime because much goes unreported (victims fearful) or unrecorded (police discretion).

items.q: Durkheim's 1897 study used social facts about which behaviour?
items.why: It's Suicide. “Crime” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Bhaskar's three layers of reality are empirical, actual and what?
items.why: It's Real. “Imagined” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Durkheim publish 'Suicide'?
items.why: The real figure is 1897. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
